definition: Convert one interface into another interface that clients expect so incompatible components can work together.
ruleOfThumb: When integration code keeps translating field names and method shapes, isolate that translation behind one adapter.

citation.author: Gamma, Helm, Johnson & Vlissides (GoF)
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

questions.prompt: What integration smell appears across these call sites?

options.text: Legacy API translation logic is duplicated in multiple places instead of being centralized behind a compatible interface
options.explanation: Both controllers know legacy naming and unit conversions. An adapter would expose app-native methods and hide the legacy shape.

options.text: The issue is that amount multiplication should use bigint or Decimal for financial precision instead of floating point arithmetic
options.explanation: Precision can matter, but the design problem here is interface mismatch leaking into many callers.

options.text: Controllers should not call payment APIs directly and must always go through GraphQL
options.explanation: Transport choice is separate. The key issue is repeated adaptation logic.

options.text: Using snake_case payload keys violates TypeScript naming conventions and should be replaced with camelCase throughout all API integration code
options.explanation: Snake_case might be required by the external service. The issue is where that incompatibility is handled.

questions.prompt: A new vendor SDK uses cents and callback-style APIs. What keeps the core checkout code clean?

options.text: Wrap the SDK behind an adapter that presents a Promise-based, domain-friendly payment interface to CheckoutService
options.explanation: CheckoutService should speak app-native types and async style. Adapter logic localizes vendor-specific naming, units, and callbacks.

options.text: Keep vendor calls inline in each service so engineers can see the exact SDK behavior everywhere
options.explanation: Visibility across many call sites increases duplication and coupling to vendor details.

options.text: Use inheritance by making CheckoutService extend vendorSdk directly so the service gains tighter integration with all vendor-provided payment capabilities
options.explanation: Inheritance does not solve interface mismatch and further couples business logic to external implementation.

options.text: The best approach is to convert all internal money types to cents to match the SDK globally
options.explanation: Global internal model changes for one integration can spread unnecessary constraints through the codebase.

questions.prompt: Which version contains the more problematic integration design?

options.text: Version A — introducing Parser hides too much and makes debugging parser behavior impossible
options.explanation: Abstraction doesn't remove debuggability; it isolates integration concerns and simplifies service logic.

options.text: Version B — application service is tied directly to legacy parser calls and conventions instead of using a compatible parser abstraction
options.explanation: B leaks legacy API choices into core service code. A lets an adapter implement Parser and contain that translation.
